methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $75M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Quaise Energy is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Carlos Araque and Matt Houde that has raised $75M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of three same-stage, same-sector private peers (Graphyte at $200M, Fourth Power at $150M, and Steady Energy at $110M) alongside a broader set of nine private companies and one public comparable (LNZA) operating in the frontier energy technology space.

Two independent triangulation methods produce convergent results. The stage-progression method applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement to the $75M total raised proxy, implying a $150M valuation. The peer-median approach across the three identified comparables yields a median valuation of $150M, with a range spanning $110M to $200M. Both methods support an identical point estimate.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $128M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $150M. The tight convergence across methods reduces execution risk in the estimate; however, material uncertainty remains around the company's technical progress toward commercial viability, the timing and magnitude of capital expenditures required for scaling, and the competitive intensity within the frontier energy sector. The peer sample size of three introduces concentration risk and limits robustness to sector-specific pivots.
